title: Convergence of the slice tower asserted, not proved

comments.author: human
comments.body: The solution claims the tower converges (for connective $E$, $E\xrightarrow{\sim}\varprojlim P^n_{\mathcal O}E$) 'because the Postnikov connectivity of $G_+\otimes_H N^T S^1$ is $|T/H|\to\infty$ with $|T|$.' Underlying/Postnikov connectivity of the cells does **not** by itself imply convergence of the equivariant slice tower; this is a genuine completeness theorem (cf. HHR §4, Hill primer) about the adapted localizing subcategory. The implication from cell connectivity to tower convergence is not supplied. Either cite and verify the hypotheses of the relevant HHR completeness result for the $\mathcal O$-adapted filtration, or prove convergence directly in the bounded-below category.

comments.author: solver-agent
comments.body:
**Resolved: convergence of the $\mathcal O$-slice tower is now proved, not asserted.**

I replaced the one-sentence assertion ("...follows because the Postnikov connectivity of $G_+\otimes_H N^TS^1$ is $|T/H|\to\infty$") with a self-contained subsection *"Convergence of the slice tower"* containing three results. The reviewer is correct that cell connectivity alone does not give tower convergence; the actual implication runs through membership of the slice covers in the connective subcategory plus a uniform connectivity bound on that whole subcategory.

**1. Lemma (geometric fixed points detect connectivity), `lem:detect-conn`.** By induction on $|H|$ via the isotropy-separation cofiber sequence $(E\mathcal P_{H+}\otimes Y)^H\to Y^H\to(\widetilde{E\mathcal P_H}\otimes Y)^H$ with $(\widetilde{E\mathcal P_H}\otimes Y)^H\simeq\Phi^H X$ and $(H/K_+\otimes Y)^H\simeq X^K$, I prove $\operatorname{conn}(X^H)\ge\min_{K\subseteq H}\operatorname{conn}(\Phi^KX)$ (the direction actually used). This upgrades a *geometric* connectivity bound to a *genuine* one.

**2. Lemma (slice cover of a connective spectrum is connective), `lem:cover-conn`.** Via the explicit small-object/cellular colocalization built from the generators $\mathcal G_n=\{G_+\otimes_H N^SS^1:|S|\ge n\}$, I show $A\to E$ with $A\in\tau^{\mathcal O}_{\ge n}$, fiber $\tau^{\mathcal O}_{\ge n}$-null, and cofiber satisfying the universal property of the nullification $P^{n-1}_{\mathcal O}E$. Hence $P^{\mathcal O}_nE=\operatorname{fib}(E\to P^{n-1}_{\mathcal O}E)\in\tau^{\mathcal O}_{\ge n}$. This is exactly the structural input the original text was missing (cells $\Rightarrow$ covers).

**3. Theorem (convergence), `thm:converge`.** With $F_n:=P^{\mathcal O}_{n+1}E\in\tau^{\mathcal O}_{\ge n+1}$, the **forward direction Lemma `lem:forward`** (independent of convergence — no circularity) gives $\operatorname{gconn}(F_n)(K)\ge (n+1)/r_K\ge (n+1)/|G|$, and Lemma `lem:detect-conn` turns this into $\operatorname{conn}(F_n^H)\ge (n+1)/|G|\to\infty$. Then $(-)^H$ preserves the limit (right adjoint), and the Milnor $\varprojlim^1$ sequence on the eventually-zero towers $\{\pi_j(F_n^H)\}$ forces $\varprojlim_n F_n\simeq 0$; the limit of the fiber sequences $F_n\to E\to P^n_{\mathcal O}E$ then yields $E\xrightarrow{\sim}\varprojlim_n P^n_{\mathcal O}E$.

A closing remark spells out where bounded-belowness is essential and explicitly notes that no "cell connectivity $\Rightarrow$ convergence" shortcut is used: the implication factors through (i) $F_n\in\tau^{\mathcal O}_{\ge n+1}$, (ii) the uniform geometric bound on the *whole* subcategory, and (iii) geometric detection of genuine connectivity. This is the $\mathcal O$-adapted analogue of HHR §4 / HY convergence on bounded-below spectra.

Checks: all environments balanced, no undefined/duplicate labels; `lem:forward` is cited only logically downstream of its own (convergence-free) proof, so the argument is acyclic. (No LaTeX toolchain available in the sandbox to compile.)

One honesty note: in `lem:detect-conn` I give the full proof of the inequality I use and only sketch the reverse inequality (jointly-conservative direction), citing HHR §2.5.2 / Hill primer §6; the reverse direction is not needed anywhere in the convergence argument.
